our_argument_summary: Section 5.16 documents the dome's real-time monitoring infrastructure: monitor.py (every 5 minutes, 39+ domains, NOAA/USGS/HeartMath/OpenSky) and pull_data.py (every 6 hours). We credit the engineering, then identify four problems: (1) adaptive tolerances that widen automatically, preventing falsification; (2) Kp < 2 escape clause on the eclipse prediction that records null instead of false when Kp >= 2 (~60% of days); (3) internal numerical disagreement — homepage -17 to -21 nT vs monitor.py -29.1 nT for Ebro; (4) blockchain timestamps observations but not predictions. The closing assessment: the infrastructure is more rigorous than the model it monitors, but manages PR rather than testing science.

holes_found.description: The '-17 to -21 nT' range attributed to the homepage cannot be verified against our archived raw text. The dome's predictions page (03-predictions.txt, V50.6) shows E001 Ebro: BOU pred -10.7 nT, W004 pred -21.7 nT — a range of roughly -10.7 to -21.7 nT, not -17 to -21 nT. The '-17 to -21' range does not appear anywhere in the seven archived raw-text files. If this number comes from the homepage HTML (which may have been updated for V51.0) or from monitor.py itself (which we haven't archived), we cannot substantiate it from our evidence. A dome defender could say: 'Show me where the homepage says -17 to -21 nT. The predictions page clearly shows -10.7 nT (BOU baseline) and -21.7 nT (W004 baseline) for Ebro.' If the range is misquoted, the '40% disagreement' claim also changes: -29.1 vs -21.7 is a 34% gap, while -29.1 vs -10.7 is a factor of 2.7. The internal contradiction exists regardless, but our specific numbers may be wrong.
holes_found.recommendation: Verify the exact '-17 to -21 nT' claim. If it comes from a V51.0 update we haven't archived, note the source and archive it. If it was imprecise paraphrasing of the W004 Ebro range (-21.7 nT), correct to the exact published number. More importantly, clarify WHICH baseline the monitor.py calculation corresponds to — the dome publishes two baselines (BOU and W004) with different results, and our review doesn't specify which one monitor.py actually uses or whether monitor.py uses a third formula entirely. The contradiction between homepage and code remains real regardless, but our presentation needs exact citations.

holes_found.description: DOCX/PDF format entirely omits Section 5.16. As flagged in SEC-5.14 and SEC-5.15 reviews, Sections 5.14-5.16 exist only in generate-html.js. The DOCX builder (build-doc-v4.js) has no corresponding content. Word document and PDF readers — the formats most likely to be shared and cited — miss the monitoring infrastructure analysis entirely. This is the fourth curmudgeon review flagging this structural gap. The monitoring infrastructure critique is one of the review's most original arguments (adaptive tolerances, escape clauses, blockchain provenance asymmetry) and is completely absent from one of the two distribution formats.
holes_found.recommendation: Priority: add Sections 5.14, 5.15, and 5.16 to build-doc-v4.js. This is now a cross-cutting structural deficiency affecting three sections of the review's strongest original content.

holes_found.description: The monitor.py code itself is not archived in raw-text/. Every specific claim in Section 5.16 — the adaptive tolerance formula, the Kp < 2 escape clause, the -18.22 × 0.95 × 1.672 computation, the 39+ prediction domains, the OpenTimestamps integration — relies on code analysis performed during earlier review sessions. If the dome author modifies or removes monitor.py, our claims become unsubstantiated. This is the same provenance gap flagged in the SEC-5.14 review, but it's especially acute for Section 5.16 because ALL FOUR of its arguments depend on specific code citations. The SEC-5.14 review correctly identified this as ironic: we criticize the dome for timestamping the wrong side of the ledger while we haven't archived our own evidence.
holes_found.recommendation: Archive monitor.py, pull_data.py, and the two GitHub Actions workflow files (monitor.yml, ecm-data-pull.yml) in raw-text/ alongside the existing webpage extracts. This is the single most important evidence preservation task for the review.

holes_found.description: The '-29.1 nT' calculation needs verification. Our section says monitor.py computes 'ΔB = −18.22 × 0.95 × 1.672 ≈ −29.1 nT'. Computing this: -18.22 × 0.95 × 1.672 = -28.93, which rounds to -28.9 or -29.0, not -29.1. The rounding error is tiny and doesn't affect the argument, but the dome's predictions page shows Ebro FSF as 1.029 (not 1.672) and baselines as -10.9 nT (BOU) and -22.24 nT (W004). Where does 1.672 come from? It doesn't match any station's FSF in the predictions page (closest is Eskdalemuir at 1.722). Either monitor.py uses a different formula/FSF than the predictions page — which would be a STRONGER version of our internal-contradiction argument — or our quote of the monitor.py formula is imprecise. Without the archived code, we can't confirm which.
holes_found.recommendation: If the FSF 1.672 does come from monitor.py and differs from the predictions page's FSF 1.029 for Ebro, highlight this as an additional internal contradiction: the predictions page and the monitoring script don't even agree on the field strength factor for the same station. If the formula was from a different station or an older version, correct the citation.

holes_found.description: The '~60% of days have Kp >= 2' claim needs a citation. This is presented as fact but no source is provided. The Kp index distribution is well-documented — NOAA's Space Weather Prediction Center publishes Kp statistics. The actual percentage depends on the averaging period and solar cycle phase. During solar maximum (which 2026 approaches), geomagnetically quiet days (Kp < 2 for all 3-hour intervals) are indeed relatively uncommon. But the claim should specify: does 'Kp >= 2' mean the daily maximum Kp, the average Kp, or any 3-hour interval? If monitor.py checks the instantaneous Kp at eclipse time, the percentage might be different from daily statistics. A dome defender could argue: 'The Kp < 2 precondition is standard practice for geomagnetic studies — you wouldn't measure eclipse effects during a storm. The 60% claim is misleading because we only need ONE quiet day during the eclipse window.'
holes_found.recommendation: Add a citation for the Kp distribution claim (NOAA SWPC publishes Kp frequency tables). Specify what 'Kp >= 2' means in the context of monitor.py (daily max, single interval, etc.). Also acknowledge the dome defender's point about storm exclusion being reasonable — the issue is not that they exclude storms, but that the RESULT of exclusion is null rather than 'test not applicable,' combined with how null results are presented to the reader.

holes_found.description: The 'created 2026-04-05' date for monitor.py is stated as fact but uncited. If monitor.py was actually created earlier and updated on that date, or if the creation date comes from a git commit that may have been amended, the provenance claim is less clear-cut. Since we criticize the dome for timestamp ambiguity, we should be precise about our own dating of their code artifacts.
holes_found.recommendation: Cite the source of the creation date (git first-commit date, file creation date in repository, etc.). If this is an approximation, say 'as of' or 'committed on' rather than 'created.'

holes_found.description: The section conflates two distinct issues under 'adaptive tolerances' without making the logical structure explicit. Issue A: tolerance = median(errors) + 2×stdev(errors) is a moving goalpost that prevents falsification. Issue B: minimum 50% tolerance floor prevents narrow predictions from ever being tested rigorously. These are separate problems with different implications. A dome defender might rebut Issue A by saying 'adaptive tolerances converge to the true precision of the measurement' (which is actually a reasonable statistical argument for uncertainty estimation). The real problem is that the tolerance adapts to the MODEL's errors, not to MEASUREMENT uncertainty — the formula uses prediction errors, not instrument noise. This distinction needs to be explicit.
holes_found.recommendation: Separate the two adaptive tolerance issues and clarify that the tolerance formula uses the model's own prediction errors (how much the model has historically been wrong) rather than the measurement instrument's uncertainty (how precisely the value can be measured). If the dome model consistently predicts values 40% too high, the tolerance grows to encompass 40% — this is not uncertainty quantification, it's retroactive loosening of success criteria.

holes_found.description: The blockchain provenance argument could be more precise. We say 'Git history can be rewritten (git rebase, force push); blockchain anchoring cannot.' While true, public repositories on GitHub have additional protections: if anyone has cloned or forked the repo, the original commit hashes are preserved. GitHub's own Event API also records push events with hashes. The argument is correct in principle but slightly overstates the practical mutability of public git history. A dome defender familiar with git could point this out.
holes_found.recommendation: Acknowledge that public git history is practically harder to rewrite without detection (especially with forks, clones, and GitHub API logs), but note that the dome's case is unusual: the repository appears to be a single-author project with no known forks at the time of analysis. The theoretical mutability of git vs. blockchain still matters in this context.

holes_found.description: The section title '5.16 The Monitoring Infrastructure' is numbered as if it's in Part 5, but it's placed after the Part 4.6 code analysis section and before Part 6 Conclusions. The numbering scheme is inconsistent: Part 4.6 is labeled 'Part 4.6' with an H1, while 5.14, 5.15, and 5.16 use H2 headings. Are these subsections of Part 5 (AI Directive Analysis) or standalone sections? The section numbering suggests they belong to Part 5, but their content is about repository code analysis, which aligns more with Part 4.6.
holes_found.recommendation: Minor organizational issue — consider whether 5.14-5.16 should be renumbered as 4.7, 4.8, etc. (since they extend the code analysis of Part 4.6) or whether Part 5's scope should be explicitly expanded to include repository infrastructure. The current numbering is confusing because these sections continue the code analysis argument rather than the AI directive argument.

stronger_arguments.description: The strongest argument not currently made: the dome's own code_analysis tags (from our curmudgeon review of 64 WINs) show that only 6 of 64 reviewed WINs actually fetch live data. But the monitoring infrastructure claims to audit '39+ prediction domains.' If 39+ domains are being audited but only 6 WINs involve live data fetches, what are the other 33+ domains checking? This discrepancy between the number of monitored domains and the number of WINs with genuine live validation deserves explicit discussion. Either the '39+' figure counts hardcoded domains (inflating the impression of rigor) or there are many domains unrelated to the 67 published WINs (monitoring something other than the claimed predictions).
sources: Current code_analysis tags: 6 live_fetch, 24 hardcoded, 34 none out of 64 reviewed

stronger_arguments.description: The section doesn't connect back to Part 4.6.1 (The Monitoring Illusion) explicitly enough. Section 5.16 describes the INFRASTRUCTURE while 4.6.1 describes the RESULTS of that infrastructure. The punchline connecting them should be: the infrastructure is sophisticated (5.16 grants this), but when you examine what the infrastructure actually does for each WIN, it's mostly checking hardcoded values against themselves (4.6.1). The gap between the engineering quality and the scientific content is the core insight — and it should be stated as a cross-reference.
sources: Section 4.6.1 analysis, SEC-3.5.6 review of 95.2% accuracy

stronger_arguments.description: The blockchain argument could be strengthened with a specific test: if the dome's prediction parameters have been stable since the OpenTimestamps anchoring began, the git history would show no force-pushes or rebases affecting prediction files. We could check this (or note that we cannot without repository access). If the prediction parameters HAVE changed since blockchain anchoring began, that would prove the asymmetry is exploitable. If they haven't, the argument becomes more theoretical. Either way, stating the empirical test makes our argument more scientific.

kernel_of_truth.description: The monitoring infrastructure represents a genuine, unusual commitment to empirical testing that is rare in alternative cosmology. Polling real data every 5 minutes, blockchain-timestamping results, and publishing the code openly are practices that most mainstream scientific projects don't even attempt at this frequency. The dome author is correct that most critics of alternative models never engage with specific predictions — the ECM's monitoring system at least creates a framework for doing so. The storm-exclusion logic (Kp < 2) is also defensible in principle — geomagnetic measurements during storms are genuinely contaminated, and any competent geophysicist would want to control for storm effects.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The gap between infrastructure sophistication and scientific content is what damns the model. Having a monitoring system that runs every 5 minutes is impressive engineering; having that system mostly check hardcoded values against themselves is theater. The adaptive tolerance formula doesn't quantify measurement uncertainty (the scientifically valid purpose) — it quantifies model failure and then redefines failure as success. The Kp escape clause doesn't just control for storms — it converts potential falsification events into null results that vanish from the record. And blockchain-timestamping observations while leaving predictions in mutable git history isn't an accident — it's optimal for claiming provenance without actually having it. The infrastructure tells us the author understands what rigorous science LOOKS like; the implementation tells us the goal is appearance, not substance.

deeper_analysis:
Section 5.16 is one of the review's most effective sections because it does something unusual: it genuinely credits the dome model before dismantling its claims. The 'this infrastructure is genuinely sophisticated' opening establishes credibility that makes the subsequent critique more devastating. This is good rhetorical practice.

However, the section has a foundational vulnerability: every specific claim depends on code we haven't archived. If the dome author were to modify monitor.py to remove the adaptive tolerance, change the eclipse escape clause, or update the FSF values, our section would be making claims about code that no longer matches our description. This is not a hypothetical risk — the dome model has gone through multiple versions (V0.4 through V51.0) and the author clearly iterates frequently.

The strongest finding is the blockchain provenance asymmetry (point 4), which is a genuinely novel observation about the epistemology of cryptographic timestamping in science. The argument that 'the strongest cryptographic proof applies to the part that needs it least' is philosophically sharp and hard to rebut. A dome defender's only response would be 'we plan to add prediction timestamping later' — which is an admission that the current system doesn't do it.

The weakest finding is the eclipse escape clause (point 2), not because the observation is wrong, but because storm exclusion is genuinely standard practice in geomagnetic research. Our argument needs to focus not on the fact of exclusion but on the asymmetry of the result: null rather than 'test postponed, will retry.' If the monitoring system recorded 'storm interference — test invalid, rescheduled for next quiet window' that would be scientific. Recording null (which disappears from aggregate statistics) is narrative management.

The dome defender's best overall rebuttal to this section: 'The monitoring infrastructure is evolving. You're critiquing version 1 of a system that will improve. Show me another alternative cosmology model with ANY monitoring infrastructure, let alone one polling real data every 5 minutes. You're punishing us for being more rigorous than our peers.' This is a genuinely strong defense — the monitoring system IS more rigorous than most alternative models, and our review should acknowledge this more prominently while maintaining that 'more rigorous than other alternative models' is a low bar that doesn't make the science valid.

Meta-observation: The irony of our review's own monitoring pipeline (curmudgeon, decider, integrity agents running on schedules) parallels the dome's monitoring infrastructure. We are doing to our own review what the dome does to its predictions — continuous automated self-checking. The difference is that our agents are designed to find and fix errors (adversarial), while the dome's system is designed to confirm predictions (confirmatory). But the structural similarity is worth being aware of for intellectual honesty.

citations_verified: OpenTimestamps as a Bitcoin blockchain anchoring service (exists and functions as described)
GitHub Actions workflows can run on cron schedules (standard GitHub feature)
INTERMAGNET as a source for geomagnetic data (standard reference)
NOAA, USGS, HeartMath GCI, OpenSky Network as data sources (all real, public APIs)
citations_failed: '-17 to -21 nT at Ebro' — cannot verify against archived raw text. Predictions page shows -10.7 (BOU) to -21.7 (W004) for Ebro.
'ΔB = −18.22 × 0.95 × 1.672 ≈ −29.1 nT' — FSF 1.672 does not match Ebro's listed FSF of 1.029 in predictions page. Source code not archived.
'~60% of days have Kp ≥ 2' — no citation provided for this statistical claim
citations_unchecked: monitor.py 'created 2026-04-05' — date not independently verifiable without repository access
'39+ prediction domains' — count not verifiable without archived monitor.py
'tolerance = median(errors) + 2×stdev(errors)' — formula not verifiable without archived monitor.py
'pass=null rather than pass=false' for Kp >= 2 — logic not verifiable without archived monitor.py
'minimum of 50%' tolerance floor — not verifiable without archived monitor.py
